title: Letter 1
summary: The letter was written to his sister Margaret Saville in St. Petersburgh, England. It was written in the hope of discovering a passage to the North Pacific Ocean around the pole of the Earth. Six years have passed since I resolved on my present undertaking. My courage and my resolution isfirm; but my hopes fluctuate, and my spirits are often depressed.

title: Letter 2
summary: A young man writes to his sister about his desire for a friend. He describes his desire as a most severe evil. He writes that he is self-educated but is more illiterate than many schoolboys of 15. He also describes his love for a lady who owes him the happiness of her life.

title: Letter 3
summary: The letter will reach England by a merchantman now on its homeward voyage from Archangel. It will reach Mrs. Saville, England, on July 7th, 17th. The letter is addressed to Margaret, her sister, and her husband. It is also addressed to Mrs Saville's mother and father.

title: Letter 4
summary: The letter was sent to Mrs Saville, England, on August 5th, 17th. It was written while the ship was surrounded by ice. A strange sight attracted the attention of the crew. A man was trapped on board the ship. His limbs were nearly frozen, and his body dreadfully emaciated by fatigue.

title: Chapter 1
summary: Elizabeth Lavenza was the daughter of a Milanese nobleman. Her mother was a German and had died on giving her birth. Her father was one of those Italians nursed in the memory of the antique glory of Italy. He became the victim of the liberty of his country and his child became an orphan and a beggar.

title: Chapter 2
summary: The author was brought up with his wife and two children in Geneva, Switzerland. He describes his childhood as one of 'harmony' and 'gladness' He writes of his childhood friend Henry Clerval, a boy of 'talent and fancy' and of the heroes of King Arthur.

title: Chapter 3
summary: The author's father, Clerval, and brother, Elizabeth and Elizabeth's friend, Henry, travelled with him to Ingolstadt to see him off to university. Henry's father was a narrow-minded trader and saw idleness and ruin in the ambition of his son. Henry felt the misfortune of being debarred from a liberal education.

title: Chapter 4
summary: The author was a pupil of M. Waldman at Ingolstadt. He became fascinated by the structure of the human frame and the natural decay and corruption of the body. The writer was too busy working in his workshop to write to his father. He was working in a slaughter-house and a dissecting room, and his work was loathsome.

title: Chapter 5
summary: Victor Frankenstein met Henry Clerval at his college in Paris. He was ill, and feared that the monster he had left in his apartment might still be there, alive and walking about. Frankenstein describes his horror at the sight of the wretch who had taken over his body. He describes his joy at seeing his father, Elizabeth, and all those scenes of home.

title: Chapter 6
summary: Clerval sent a letter to his cousin Victor, who had been ill. The letter was written in the handwriting of his cousin's wife, Elizabeth. Clerval wrote that Victor was 'getting better' and that he would soon return to his family. The author was on a tour of his native Germany. He describes how he was filled with unbridled joy and hilarity.

title: Chapter 7
summary: Victor Frankenstein's mother wrote to her son, asking him to return and be her comforter. Frankenstein went to Geneva to find out what had happened to his son. He describes the moment he realized that the creature he had created was the murderer of his brother. The author describes how he tried to pursue the creature, but it would have been in vain.

title: Chapter 8
summary: Justine was accused of the murder of a child at the home of her aunt. The child's body was found in the village of Chêne. Her defence was that she had been out the whole of the night on which the murder was committed. Justine says she confessed to the murder in order to get absolution. She says she is now 'doomed to ignominy and perdition'

title: Chapter 9
summary: Justine died, she rested, and I was alive. The blood flowed in my veins, but a weight of despair and remorse pressed on my heart. I shunned the face of man; all sound of joy or complacency was torture to me. "I lived in daily fear lest the monster whom I had created should perpetrate some new wickedness"

title: Chapter 10
summary: The author describes a journey to the summit of Mont Blanc in the Alps. He says he was overcome with rage and hatred when he saw the figure of the man he had murdered. The author describes how he threatened to kill the man with his arm. He also describes his encounter with the devil.

title: Chapter 11
summary: “I felt light, and hunger, and thirst, and darkness; innumerable sounds rang in my ears, and on. all sides various scents saluted me’ “I was a poor, helpless, miserable wretch; I knew, and could notdistinguish, nothing; but feeling pain invade me on all sides, I sat                down and wept” “The whole village was roused; some fled, some attacked me, until Iescaped to the open country and fearfully took refuge in a low hovel’

title: Chapter 12
summary: ‘I longed to join them, but dared not. I saw no cause for their unhappiness, But I was deeply affected by it. If such lovely creatures weremiserable, it was less strange that I, an imperfect and solitary being, should be wretched’ ‘I imagined that they would be disgusted, until, by my gentle demeanor and conciliating words, I should first win their favour and then their love’

title: Chapter 13
summary: “I shall relate events that impressed me with feelings which, from what I had been, have made me what I am” “Spring advanced rapidly; the weather became fine and the skies were cloudless. It surprised me that what before was desert and gloomy should now bloom with the most beautiful flowers and verdure. My senses were gratified and refreshed by a thousand scents of delight and a thousand sights of beauty”

title: Chapter 14
summary: “Some time elapsed before I learned the history of my friends. It was a story which could not fail to impress itself deeply on my mind, unfolding in a number of circumstances, each interesting and wonderful to one so utterly inexperienced as I was.” ‘‘Safie’ was the name of the young woman who was killed by her lover’s wife. ‘Safia’ meant ‘savior’

title: Chapter 15
summary: ‘Such was the history of my beloved cottagers. It impressed me deeply. I learned, from the views of social life which it developed, to admire                their virtues and to deprecate the vices of mankind’ ‘I persuaded myself that when they should become acquainted with my admiration of their virtues they would overlook my personal deformity' ‘They are kind—they are the most excellent creatures in the world; but, unfortunately, they are prejudiced against me'

title: Chapter 16
summary: “Cursed, cursed creator! Why did I live? Why, in that instant, did I not extinguish the spark of existence which you had so wantonly bestowed? I know not; despair had not yet taken possession of me; myfeelings were those of rage and revenge. I could with pleasure have destroyed the cottage and its inhabitants and glutted myself with their shrieks and misery” “I cannot describe the suspense of this suspense. The inside of the cottage was dark, and I heard no motion’

title: Chapter 17
summary: The being said: “You must create a female for me with whom I can live in the.interchange of those sympathies necessary for my being.” “I do refuse it,” I replied; “and no torture shall ever extort a                consent from me’ ‘You may torture me, but I will never consent,’ replied the fiend. ‘I will go to the vast wilds of South America. My food is not that of man’

title: Chapter 18
summary: Victor's father asked his son if he wanted to marry his cousin Elizabeth. Victor replied that he was still unhappy and avoided society. Victor's father then asked him if he would like to marry Elizabeth. He replied that it was not necessary. A letter to his daughter from her father tells her of his desire to marry her.

title: Chapter 19
summary: Author spent two months in Scotland and the north-west of England. Henry VIII wrote of his visit to the Orkney islands of Scotland. He describes how he lived in squalor in a tiny cottage in the remotest part of the island. The author describes the horror of his work as 'filthy' and 'detestable'

title: Chapter 20
summary: The story of the murder of a man in Ireland is told in the novel "The Death of a Gentleman" by James Joyce. The story is told from the point-of-view of a young man who was taken to Ireland by a magistrate. The author describes the man's escape from Ireland and his ordeal in the aftermath of his death.

title: Chapter 21
summary: The author describes the moment he saw the body of his friend Henry Clerval. He says he felt the fingers of the monster already grasping his neck, and screamed aloud with agony and terror. The author describes his ordeal as a 'dream' and how he was nursed back to health by a nurse and a doctor. He describes his journey to Geneva after the death of his mother.

title: Chapter 22
summary: Victor's father did not know the origin of his sufferings and sought erroneous methods to remedy the incurable ill. Elizabeth wrote to Victor that she was worried that Victor would regret their union. Victor writes back to Elizabeth, asking her to tell him how she feels about him. “I fear, my beloved girl,” I said, “little happiness remains for us on earth”

title: Chapter 23
summary: The author recounts the death of his wife and the murder of his father. He describes the moment he realised that his wife had been killed by a monster. He then describes how he tried to find his son-in-law's body but was unable to. The story is too connected to be mistaken for a dream, and I have no motive for falsehood.

title: Chapter 24
summary: The author travelled to the North Pole to kill a dæmon. He was pursued by a fiend who left him marks on trees and stones. The journey was arduous, but he eventually reached the shore of the ocean. He describes the joy he felt when he saw the ocean and its waves. Frankenstein was confined to his bed as his illness increased.
